content.problemStatement:
Abhi is a salesman. He was given two types of candies, which he is selling in NNN different cities.

For the prices of the candies to be valid, Abhi's boss laid down the following condition:


A given type of candy must have **distinct** prices in all NNN cities.
In his excitement, Abhi wrote down the prices of both the candies on the same page and in random order instead of writing them on different pages. Now he is asking for your help to find out if the prices he wrote are valid or not.

You are given an array AAA of size 2N2N2N. Find out whether it is possible to split AAA into two arrays, each of length NNN, such that both arrays consist of distinct elements.

Both arrays can have distinct elements only if no element in the original array is repeated more than twice.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN, denoting the number of cities
The second line contains 2N2N2N space-separated integers A1,A2,…,A2NA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{2N}A1​,A2​,…,A2N​ — the elements of the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case output the answer on a new line — `Yes` if the given array represents a valid list of prices, and `No` otherwise.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, `Yes`, `YES`, and `yEs` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^3
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (the number of cities). The second line contains 2 N space‑separated integers A1 … A2N – the recorded candy prices.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Yes" if the array can be split into two arrays of length N each having all distinct elements, otherwise output "No".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: frequencies are {4:2,8:1,6:1,7:1,3:1} – all ≤2 → Yes.
Test case 2: value 8 appears three times → No.
Test case 3: all values appear once → Yes.
Test case 4: value 8 appears three times → No.
The only price 5 appears exactly twice, which can be placed once in each candy array.
All six prices are distinct, so we can arbitrarily assign three of them to each candy.

hidden.label: general case 1 – mix of singles and doubles

solution.approach:
The condition that both resulting arrays must contain distinct elements is equivalent to requiring that no price appears more than twice in the combined list. If any value occurs three or more times, at least one of the two final arrays would contain a duplicate, making the split impossible. Conversely, when every value appears at most twice we can always distribute the occurrences: each value that appears twice is placed once in each array, and values that appear once are assigned arbitrarily to the array that still needs elements. Hence the problem reduces to checking whether the maximum frequency of any element exceeds 2.
Algorithm per test case:
1. Read N and the 2 N numbers.
2. Count frequencies using a hash map (or by sorting and scanning).
3. If any frequency > 2, answer "No"; otherwise answer "Yes".
The algorithm runs in O(2 N) time and O(distinct values) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (linear in the size of the array).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(D) where D is the number of distinct values in the test case (at most 2 N).
